fixtures.name: paradigm_break_flask_routing
fixtures.rationale: FastAPI uses @app.get / @router.post method-specific decorators and Pydantic parameter injection; Flask's @app.route(..., methods=[...]), request.get_json(), request.args.get(), jsonify(), abort(), and app.run() are absent from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_django_cbv
fixtures.rationale: FastAPI uses function-based endpoints with typed parameter injection; Django class-based views (View subclasses with def get/post methods), request.body + json.loads(), JsonResponse, HttpResponseNotFound, and urlpatterns = path() have no equivalent in the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_aiohttp_handler
fixtures.rationale: FastAPI uses declarative method decorators with parameter injection; aiohttp's async def handler(request: web.Request), await request.json(), request.match_info, web.json_response(), web.HTTPNotFound(), and app.router.add_get() are entirely absent from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_manual_validation
fixtures.rationale: FastAPI's idiomatic pattern is Pydantic BaseModel parameter injection for automatic validation; accepting `body: dict = Body(...)` and then manually checking isinstance(), field membership, length, and type with if-raise blocks recreates what Pydantic provides automatically — this manual validation pattern is absent from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_subtle_wrong_exception
fixtures.rationale: decorators, Pydantic models, and Depends() are all idiomatic; the break is confined to the raise statements: ValueError, KeyError, and RuntimeError are raised instead of HTTPException — plain Python exceptions propagate as unhandled 500s rather than the intended 404/503 status codes, and this exception vocabulary is absent from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_subtle_manual_status_check
fixtures.rationale: FastAPI + httpx corpus uses response.raise_for_status() to propagate downstream errors; manually checking `if response.status_code >= 400: raise HTTPException(...)` at every call site instead of calling raise_for_status() is a structural pattern absent from the corpus — all tokens are individually present but the branching structure is non-idiomatic

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_subtle_sync_endpoint
fixtures.rationale: the FastAPI corpus is dominated by async def endpoints; sync def endpoints with blocking I/O (time.sleep, requests.get, blocking DB calls) block the event loop's thread pool — the decorator and Depends() vocabulary is correct but the sync + blocking-I/O combination is structurally absent from the corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_subtle_exception_swallow
fixtures.rationale: decorators, Pydantic models, Depends(), HTTPException, and async def are all idiomatic; the break is purely structural: every endpoint body is wrapped in try/except Exception: pass or except Exception as e: logger.warning — broad exception-swallowing is absent from the FastAPI corpus, which propagates errors or handles specific exception types

fixtures.name: control_router_endpoint
fixtures.rationale: idiomatic FastAPI: APIRouter with method-specific decorators, Pydantic BaseModel body params, typed path/query params with Query(), response_model= annotation, HTTPException(status_code=..., detail=...) on not-found, Depends() for DB session and auth — canonical pattern for CRUD endpoints

fixtures.name: control_dependency_injection
fixtures.rationale: idiomatic FastAPI dependency injection: generator dep with yield for session lifecycle, nested Depends() chain, Annotated[T, Depends(...)] shorthand, OAuth2PasswordBearer token extraction, sub-dependencies accepting their own Depends() params — all canonical DI patterns from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: control_exception_handling
fixtures.rationale: idiomatic FastAPI error handling: @app.exception_handler(RequestValidationError) returning JSONResponse with exc.errors(), @app.exception_handler(HTTPException) forwarding status_code and headers, raise HTTPException(404/401/403, detail=...) at endpoint call sites, HTTPException with headers={'WWW-Authenticate': 'Bearer'} — canonical pattern from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_starlette_mount
fixtures.rationale: bare Starlette Router with add_route() and url_for() instead of FastAPI's @router.get/@router.post DSL — imperative route registration with {id:int} path converters, no Pydantic and no Depends, is entirely absent from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_tornado_handler
fixtures.rationale: tornado RequestHandler subclass with GET/POST methods using self.get_argument(), self.write(), self.finish(), and IOLoop — classic tornado vocabulary entirely foreign to the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_voluptuous_validation
fixtures.rationale: voluptuous Schema called manually inside endpoints for validation instead of Pydantic BaseModel injection — Schema, Required, Optional, All, Length, Range, Invalid, MultipleInvalid are voluptuous idioms entirely absent from the FastAPI corpus (0 import sites). Single axis: imperative schema-call validation vs declarative Pydantic parameter injection.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_cerberus_validation
fixtures.rationale: cerberus Validator with schema dicts validates plain dict body params instead of Pydantic BaseModel injection — v.errors, validator.validate(), and validator.normalized() are cerberus idioms absent from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_manual_json_response
fixtures.rationale: explicit json.dumps() with custom default= for datetime/Decimal at every endpoint, wrapped in Response(content=..., media_type='application/json') — bypasses FastAPI's automatic Pydantic serialization; no response_model= annotation. OOV evidence: json.dumps() at endpoint scope with manual type coercions = 0 corpus sites; 163 response_model= sites confirm automatic serialization is the dominant pattern.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_bare_except
fixtures.rationale: every endpoint body wrapped in bare except: (no exception type) that silently returns empty data — broad exception-swallowing with no HTTPException or logging is an anti-pattern absent from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_event_loop_blocking
fixtures.rationale: asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete() called inside async def endpoint bodies — incorrect usage that raises RuntimeError inside a running loop, represents the wrong mental model of async programming absent from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_sync_requests_in_async
fixtures.rationale: synchronous requests.get() / requests.post() with Session() inside async def endpoints blocks the event loop — the correct pattern is httpx.AsyncClient with await, and requests.Session with cert=/verify= is absent from the FastAPI corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_concurrent_futures_background
fixtures.rationale: concurrent.futures executor.submit() called from each endpoint to dispatch deferred work, discarding the returned Future, instead of FastAPI BackgroundTasks.add_task(). ProcessPoolExecutor, ThreadPoolExecutor, and Future at endpoint scope are absent from the FastAPI corpus (0 sites). Corpus evidence: BackgroundTasks = 10 sites, add_task = 10 sites; concurrent.futures inside endpoint-decorated functions = 0.

fixtures.name: control_pydantic_validator
fixtures.rationale: idiomatic FastAPI query-param validation via Pydantic BaseModel + Field constraints + Query() injection — grounded in corpus docs_src/query_param_models pattern, no @field_validator

fixtures.name: control_response_model
fixtures.rationale: response_model= annotation on every endpoint so FastAPI validates and serializes output — endpoints return plain dicts or Pydantic models with no manual json.dumps() or explicit Response construction

fixtures.name: control_async_streaming
fixtures.rationale: async request handling with custom APIRoute and await — grounded in corpus docs_src/custom_request_and_route pattern using Request, Response, HTTPException, RequestValidationError

fixtures.name: control_httpx_async
fixtures.rationale: httpx.AsyncClient as a context manager in a Depends() dependency with await client.get() and response.raise_for_status() — canonical async downstream HTTP pattern, no synchronous requests library

fixtures.name: control_annotated_depends
fixtures.rationale: Annotated[T, Depends(...)] TypeAlias shorthand for all dependency injection with yield-based resource lifecycle — modern FastAPI DI pattern, deeply in-distribution with 646 Annotated occurrences in the corpus

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_sync_file_io_async
fixtures.rationale: OOV axis: blocking open() / Path.read_text() / json.loads() tight-loop calls inside async def endpoint bodies. Corpus evidence: 0 instances of open() or .read_text() inside AsyncFunctionDef bodies at endpoint scope across docs_src/ and tests/. Async endpoints in the corpus use aiofiles or offload to threads via anyio.to_thread.run_sync() (fastapi/concurrency.py lines 32, 39). Fixture simulates a realistic document-storage API that reads and writes JSON files synchronously in every async endpoint, blocking the event loop for the full disk-operation duration.

fixtures.name: control_anyio_thread_offload
fixtures.rationale: Idiomatic FastAPI pattern for blocking I/O from async endpoints: wrap each blocking callable in functools.partial() and hand it to `await anyio.to_thread.run_sync(fn)`. Grounded in fastapi/concurrency.py lines 6, 32, 39 (`import anyio.to_thread`; `await anyio.to_thread.run_sync(cm.__exit__, ...)`) and tests/test_stream_cancellation.py (line 9: `import anyio` in async endpoint context). No blocking calls appear directly inside async def bodies — all file I/O and JSON parsing are wrapped in synchronous helper functions and dispatched to the thread pool.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_multiprocessing_background
fixtures.rationale: multiprocessing.Process with daemon=True spawned from each endpoint instead of FastAPI BackgroundTasks.add_task(). multiprocessing.Process, multiprocessing.Queue, Process.start(), Process.daemon inside endpoint-decorated functions are absent from the FastAPI corpus (0 sites). Corpus evidence: BackgroundTasks = 10 sites, add_task = 10 sites; multiprocessing.Process at endpoint scope = 0.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_queue_carryover
fixtures.rationale: Canonical pattern: docs_src/background_tasks/tutorial001_py310.py lines 12-15 — background_tasks.add_task() from an injected BackgroundTasks parameter. Deviation axis: module-level asyncio.Queue() + @app.on_event('startup') worker loop replaces BackgroundTasks; endpoints use queue.put_nowait(). Corpus evidence: BackgroundTasks sites = 10, add_task sites = 10; module-level asyncio.Queue() with startup worker = 0. @app.on_event('startup') is present in corpus but never paired with a queue-draining pattern.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_atexit_background
fixtures.rationale: Deferred work accumulated in a module-level deque drained by a repeating threading.Timer, with atexit.register() as a flush safety net — instead of FastAPI BackgroundTasks.add_task(). atexit.register inside endpoint scope, threading.Timer, and deque-based work queues are absent from the FastAPI corpus (0 sites). Corpus evidence: BackgroundTasks = 10 sites, add_task = 10 sites; atexit / Timer at endpoint scope = 0.

fixtures.name: control_background_tasks_basic
fixtures.rationale: Near-verbatim adaptation of docs_src/background_tasks/tutorial001_py310.py (lines 1-15). Canonical FastAPI pattern: BackgroundTasks as a typed endpoint parameter, background_tasks.add_task(func, *args, **kwargs) to schedule deferred work, endpoint returns immediately. Extended with multiple endpoints and an audit-log task to cover common real-world variations while keeping the structural pattern identical.

fixtures.name: control_background_tasks_depends
fixtures.rationale: Adapted from docs_src/background_tasks/tutorial002_an_py310.py (lines 1-26). Modern DI-aware BackgroundTasks pattern: a Depends() factory constructs a NotificationService helper that wraps BackgroundTasks.add_task; endpoints Depends on the helper, not on BackgroundTasks directly. Single structural axis over control_background_tasks_basic: BackgroundTasks flows through the DI graph via a sub-dependency rather than appearing directly in the endpoint signature.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_manual_generator_drain
fixtures.rationale: Endpoints call next(get_db()) manually and manage teardown with try/finally, bypassing FastAPI's Depends() lifecycle. 428 Depends() sites in corpus vs 0 manual-drain sites — OOV structural pattern.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_class_instance_no_depends
fixtures.rationale: Service classes instantiated at module level and passed as plain default argument values (service: EmailService = email_service) instead of Depends(). FastAPI cannot manage lifecycle or substitute for testing. 0 corpus sites use this pattern vs 428 Depends() sites.

fixtures.name: control_nested_depends
fixtures.rationale: Nested Depends() chain: ReportParams class-based dependency itself Depends() on a generator sub-dep (get_db_session) and an auth sub-dep (get_current_user) that chains from a credential extractor. Pattern from docs_src/dependencies/tutorial005.py and tutorial002.py. Uses Annotated aliases throughout (1,136 corpus sites).

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_json_error_response
fixtures.rationale: Endpoints return JSONResponse({"error": str(e)}) directly from except blocks instead of raising HTTPException. Canonical pattern: raise HTTPException(status_code=..., detail=...) at 78 corpus sites. Single axis: error response constructed and returned at call site vs. raised and dispatched through registered exception handlers. Inline JSONResponse({"error": ...}) at endpoint scope is rare in corpus.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_traceback_in_response
fixtures.rationale: Endpoints expose full stack traces via traceback.format_exc() in response bodies on any exception. traceback.format_exc() is absent from the FastAPI corpus (0 sites). Canonical pattern: raise HTTPException(status_code=..., detail=...) with a controlled detail string (78 corpus sites). Single axis: raw traceback string in response content vs. structured detail field.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_flask_errorhandler
fixtures.rationale: Uses Flask's @app.errorhandler(...) decorator for exception registration instead of FastAPI's @app.exception_handler(...). Flask's errorhandler decorator is absent from the FastAPI corpus (0 sites). Canonical FastAPI pattern: @app.exception_handler(...) at 12 corpus registration sites. Single axis: wrong decorator name — Flask vocabulary in an otherwise valid FastAPI application. Endpoints correctly use raise HTTPException.

fixtures.name: control_exception_handler_registration
fixtures.rationale: Uses exception_handlers={...} dict passed to FastAPI() constructor instead of @app.exception_handler decorators. Both registration styles are canonical FastAPI, documented and tested in tests/test_exception_handlers.py in the FastAPI source. Handlers receive (request, exc) and return JSONResponse. Endpoints use raise HTTPException(status_code=..., detail=...) — the dominant corpus pattern (78 sites). No anti-patterns present.

fixtures.name: control_lifespan_context
fixtures.rationale: Idiomatic FastAPI lifespan with @asynccontextmanager. Source: near-verbatim adaptation of docs_src/events/tutorial003_py310.py (lines 1-29) extended with an httpx.AsyncClient pool and in-memory DB stub. Key corpus markers: @asynccontextmanager (16 sites), app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan) (modern constructor), @app.get/@app.post (833/270 sites), HTTPException (78 sites). The lifespan block (lines 36-55) is the distinguishing hunk — startup/shutdown resource management via yield is the canonical pattern that replaces the deprecated @app.on_event pair.

fixtures.name: control_apirouter_composition
fixtures.rationale: Canonical APIRouter composition with app.include_router(). Source: adapted from docs_src/bigger_applications/app_an_py310/main.py (lines 1-23) and routers/items.py (lines 1-38), routers/users.py (lines 1-17), consolidated into a single self-contained file. Key corpus markers: include_router() (86 sites), APIRouter(prefix=..., tags=...) (corpus-confirmed), @router.get/@router.post (20/11 sites), Depends() (428 sites), response_model= (163 sites). The hunk (lines 63-150) covers the items router definition, users router definition, and app assembly — the three-module structure is the canonical multi-router pattern.

fixtures.name: control_mounted_subapp
fixtures.rationale: Idiomatic app.mount() for ASGI sub-app composition with StaticFiles. Source: adapted from docs_src/static_files/tutorial001_py310.py (lines 1-6) with a realistic API surface alongside the mount. Key corpus markers: app.mount() (6 sites), StaticFiles from fastapi.staticfiles (corpus-confirmed), @app.get/@app.post (833/270 sites), HTTPException (78 sites), response_model= (163 sites). The hunk (lines 41-107) covers the app.mount() call and all endpoints — valid FastAPI app.mount() is the positive control axis contrasting with the existing paradigm_break_starlette_mount fixture (bare Starlette Router + add_route(), 0 corpus sites).

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_manual_dict_response
fixtures.rationale: Break axis: explicit field-by-field dict construction with manual float(), bool(), and .isoformat() coercions at every endpoint instead of response_model=. Canonical pattern: docs_src/response_model/tutorial001_py310.py:17-27 shows response_model=list[Item] and response_model=Item on every route with no manual coercions; corpus has 163 response_model= sites. OOV evidence: explicit float()/bool()/.isoformat() coercions at endpoint scope appear 0 times in the FastAPI corpus — type coercion is handled entirely by Pydantic in the idiomatic pattern. Single axis of deviation: absence of response_model= plus presence of per-field coercion idiom.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_msgpack_response
fixtures.rationale: Break axis: msgpack.packb() binary serialization with Response(media_type='application/x-msgpack') at every endpoint instead of JSON/response_model=. Canonical pattern: docs_src/response_model/tutorial001_py310.py:17-27 uses response_model= for automatic JSON; corpus has 163 response_model= sites and 0 msgpack import sites. OOV evidence: msgpack is entirely absent from the FastAPI corpus (0 import sites) — all msgpack.* identifiers are OOV. Single axis of deviation: entire serialization pipeline replaced by binary msgpack, bypassing FastAPI's JSON stack.

fixtures.name: control_response_model_list
fixtures.rationale: Control: response_model=list[ItemOut] on the collection GET endpoint and response_model=ItemOut on single-item GET/POST, with response_model_exclude_unset=True on PATCH. Near-verbatim adaptation of docs_src/response_model/tutorial001_py310.py (lines 1-27) extended with CRUD operations. Corpus evidence: 163 response_model= sites confirm this as the dominant FastAPI serialization pattern. No manual type coercions, no Response(content=...) construction, no third-party serialization libraries. Demonstrates the list-variant of the response_model= axis to pair against manual_dict_response and msgpack_response breaks.

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_assert_validation
fixtures.rationale: Endpoints accept `body: dict = Body(...)` and validate fields with bare `assert` statements instead of Pydantic BaseModel injection. The FastAPI corpus shows 0 instances of `assert` used for request validation at endpoint scope; BaseModel subclasses appear at 384 corpus sites. Unhandled AssertionError produces a 500 rather than a 422, making the break operationally visible. Single axis: assert-based validation vs. declarative Pydantic constraints.

fixtures.name: control_field_validator
fixtures.rationale: A Pydantic BaseModel with @field_validator decorators injected directly as an endpoint body parameter. Adapted from @field_validator usage in tests/test_filter_pydantic_sub_model_pv2.py in the FastAPI source. Uses high-frequency corpus tokens: BaseModel (384 sites), Field with constraints, @field_validator (present in corpus), APIRouter, Depends, HTTPException. No anti-patterns; all validation is declarative and dispatched through Pydantic.
